title: Plant Pot Size Calculator 2026 | Hacé Cuentas
h1: Find Your Ideal Plant Pot Size
description: Calculate the perfect pot diameter based on root size and plant type. Free and accurate.
intro: The Plant Pot Size Calculator helps gardeners choose the right container diameter and volume for any plant based on its current root ball size and growth type. The core rule is simple: **pot diameter = root ball diameter + 2–4 inches (5–10 cm)** for most transplants. Choosing the wrong pot size is one of the leading causes of overwatering, root rot, and stunted growth. Use this tool whenever you're repotting a houseplant, starting seeds in containers, or setting up a container vegetable garden to get the exact pot diameter and volume your plant needs to thrive.
keyTakeaway: **Pot Diameter = Root Ball Diameter + 2–4 in (5–10 cm); Pot Volume (L) ≈ π × (r²) × h**, where r = pot radius in cm and h = pot depth in cm. A 22 cm pot holds ~5 L and suits a 15 cm root ball perfectly.
useCases: Repotting a potbound fiddle-leaf fig whose roots are visibly circling the bottom of a 20 cm nursery pot
Choosing the right container for a tomato plant that needs at least a 12–15 L (5-gallon) pot to produce full-size fruit
Sizing a succulent arrangement pot for a 4 cm cactus root ball that needs only a 8–10 cm diameter pot to prevent waterlogging
Scaling up containers for a balcony herb garden where mint, basil, and rosemary each require different pot volumes based on root spread
Determining the correct grow bag size for cannabis or pepper plants in an indoor hydroponic or soil-based setup
explanation:
## How It's Calculated

The recommended pot diameter is derived from the plant's current root ball diameter plus a growth buffer. The buffer varies by plant type:

```
Pot Diameter (cm) = Root Ball Diameter (cm) + Buffer (cm)

Buffer by plant type:
  Leafy / tropical houseplants  → +5 to +7 cm  (~2–3 in)
  Succulents / cacti            → +2 to +4 cm  (~1–1.5 in)
  Fruiting vegetables (tomato)  → +10 to +15 cm (~4–6 in)
  Trees / shrubs                → +15 to +20 cm (~6–8 in)
  Seedlings / herbs             → +4 to +6 cm  (~1.5–2.5 in)

Pot Volume (liters) = π × r² × h
  where r = pot radius (cm), h = pot depth (cm)

Approximation using diameter (D) and height ≈ 0.8 × D (standard tapered pot):
  Volume (L) ≈ π × (D/2)² × (0.8 × D) / 1000
```

**Example:** Root ball = 15 cm, leafy plant → Pot D = 15 + 7 = 22 cm  
Volume = π × 11² × (0.8 × 22) / 1000 ≈ 3.14159 × 121 × 17.6 / 1000 ≈ **6.7 L** (manufacturer ratings often list ~5 L for a standard tapered 22 cm pot due to thicker walls and drainage space).

---

## Reference Table

| Plant Type | Root Ball Ø (cm) | Recommended Pot Ø (cm) | Approx. Volume | Common Container |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Small succulent / cactus | 3–6 | 8–10 | 0.3–0.5 L | 4 in nursery pot |
| Herb (basil, parsley) | 5–8 | 10–14 | 0.5–1.5 L | 4–6 in pot |
| Pothos / philodendron | 10–15 | 15–22 | 2–6 L | 6–8 in pot |
| Fiddle-leaf fig | 20–30 | 28–38 | 10–25 L | 10–14 in pot |
| Tomato (full size) | 15–20 | 30–38 | 12–20 L | 5-gallon grow bag |
| Pepper plant | 12–18 | 25–30 | 8–12 L | 3–5 gallon pot |
| Dwarf citrus tree | 25–35 | 40–50 | 30–50 L | 15–20 gallon pot |
| Full shrub / small tree | 35–50 | 55–70 | 60–120 L | 25–45 gallon pot |

---

## Typical Cases

### Case 1: Repotting a Pothos (leafy houseplant)
- Current root ball diameter: **12 cm**
- Buffer for leafy plant: **+6 cm**
- Recommended pot: **18 cm Ø** (standard 7-inch pot)
- Volume: π × 9² × 14.4 / 1000 ≈ **3.7 L**
- Result: A 4 L / 7-inch pot is a perfect fit. Going up to a 24 cm pot would leave too much wet soil around the roots, increasing rot risk.

### Case 2: Container Tomato (fruiting vegetable)
- Current root ball diameter: **15 cm** (transplant seedling)
- Buffer for fruiting vegetable: **+15 cm**
- Recommended pot: **30 cm Ø minimum** (5-gallon / ~19 L)
- The USDA and extension services consistently recommend a minimum of **5 gallons (19 L)** for indeterminate tomatoes and **3 gallons (11 L)** for determinate/dwarf varieties.
- Underpotting tomatoes = water stress, blossom drop, and yields cut by 30–50%.

### Case 3: Repotting a Fiddle-Leaf Fig
- Current root ball: **22 cm Ø**, roots circling at base
- Buffer: **+8 cm** (larger tropical)
- Recommended pot: **30 cm Ø** (12-inch pot, ~14 L)
- Never jump more than **2 pot sizes (4–5 cm)** at once for figs — overpotting shocks the root system and delays re-establishment by 4–8 weeks.

---

## Common Errors

1. **Going too big too fast:** Choosing a pot 10+ cm larger than the root ball leaves a large volume of constantly wet soil with no roots to absorb moisture — the leading cause of root rot in houseplants. Always upsize by 2–5 cm max per repotting cycle.

2. **Ignoring plant type for succulents:** Succulents and cacti are often planted in pots 3–4× too large. Their roots are shallow and thin; a pot barely 2 cm wider than the root ball is ideal. Excess soil stays wet for weeks, which is fatal to most succulents.

3. **Using diameter when you need volume (or vice versa):** A 30 cm Ø pot sounds like it might work for a tomato, but a shallow 30 cm decorative bowl might hold only 4 L — far less than the 19 L minimum needed. Always check both diameter **and** depth/volume.

4. **Forgetting drainage:** A pot of the correct size with no drainage hole will behave like a pot 2–3 sizes too large in terms of moisture retention. All volume estimates assume at least one drainage hole in the base.

5. **Measuring the pot, not the root ball:** The calculator uses **current root ball diameter**, not the nursery pot diameter. Roots may fill only 60–70% of the existing pot; always unpot and measure the root mass directly for an accurate result.

---

## Related Calculators

Since no internal related slugs were provided, explore other gardening and home tools on [Hacé Cuentas](/) for more plant and garden calculations.

faq.q: How much bigger should a new pot be than the current one?
faq.a: For most houseplants, go up **2–3 cm (1 inch) in radius, or 4–6 cm (2 in) in diameter** per repotting. Fast growers like pothos or philodendron can handle a 5–7 cm jump. Slow growers like cacti should move up only 2–3 cm. Going too large causes the surrounding soil to stay waterlogged, promoting fungal root rot.

faq.q: What pot size do I need for a tomato plant?
faq.a: University cooperative extension programs (e.g., UC Davis, Penn State Extension) recommend a **minimum of 5 gallons (≈19 L, 30 cm Ø)** for standard indeterminate tomatoes and **3 gallons (≈11 L, 25 cm Ø)** for compact/determinate varieties. Underpotting reduces fruit yield by 30–50% due to restricted root volume and accelerated moisture loss.

faq.q: Does pot shape (round vs. square) affect the volume calculation?
faq.a: Yes. A round pot with D = 25 cm and h = 22 cm holds about **10.8 L** (π × 12.5² × 22 / 1000). A square pot with the same 25 cm side and 22 cm depth holds **13.75 L** (25 × 25 × 22 / 1000). Square pots provide ~20–25% more soil volume for the same footprint, which is advantageous for moisture-hungry plants like tomatoes or bananas.

faq.q: How deep should a pot be compared to its diameter?
faq.a: Standard nursery pots have a depth-to-diameter ratio of about **0.75–0.85×**. For deep-rooted plants (carrots, dahlias, roses), depth should equal or exceed the diameter. For shallow-rooted plants (succulents, lettuce, strawberries), a depth of **0.4–0.6× the diameter** is sufficient and prevents excessive soil volume from retaining moisture.

faq.q: Can I use gallons instead of liters to size my pot?
faq.a: Absolutely. The conversion is **1 US gallon = 3.785 L**. Standard US pot sizes in gallons: 1-gal ≈ 3.8 L (15 cm Ø), 2-gal ≈ 7.6 L (19 cm Ø), 5-gal ≈ 18.9 L (30 cm Ø), 15-gal ≈ 56.8 L (46 cm Ø), 25-gal ≈ 94.6 L (56 cm Ø). Most US garden centers label pots in gallons, while European and metric markets use liters.

faq.q: How often should I repot a plant, and how do I know it's time?
faq.a: Most houseplants need repotting every **1–3 years**. Signs it's time: roots visibly growing out of drainage holes, roots circling the interior pot wall (root-bound), soil drying out within 1–2 days of watering, or slowed/stunted growth despite adequate light and fertilizer. Spring is the ideal time to repot — plants enter active growth and recover faster from root disturbance.

faq.q: Does pot material affect how I should size a container?
faq.a: Yes. **Terracotta (clay) pots** are porous and lose moisture through their walls — size up slightly (2–3 cm more) or water more frequently. **Plastic and glazed ceramic pots** retain moisture longer — stick to the minimum recommended diameter to avoid overwatering. **Fabric grow bags** allow air pruning of roots and dry out faster, so size up 1–2 gallons compared to a plastic pot for the same plant.

faq.q: Is there a risk of a pot being too large for seedlings?
faq.a: Yes — this is called **overpotting**. Seedlings have tiny, delicate root systems. Placing a seedling in a 10 L pot means 95%+ of the soil has no roots drawing moisture, staying wet for days and encouraging damping-off fungal disease. Start seeds in **cell trays or 5–7 cm (2–3 in) pots** and step up progressively, following a 2–4 cm diameter increase per stage until the root system fills each pot within 4–6 weeks.

sources.name: USDA National Gardening Association – Container Vegetable Gardening
NIST – U.S. Metric Guide for Federal Agencies (Unit conversions: gallons to liters)
